2123 ///
2124 /// Calling this method is a valid operation only if all of the following
2125 /// conditions are true: 1) The process was launched, and not attached to.
2126 /// 2) The process was not launched with eLaunchFlagDisableSTDIO. 3) The
2127 /// process was launched without supplying a valid file path
2128 /// for STDOUT.
2129 ///
2130 /// Note that the implementation will probably need to start a read thread
2131 /// in the background to make sure that the pipe is drained and the STDOUT
2132 /// buffered appropriately, to prevent the process from deadlocking trying
2133 /// to write to a full buffer.
2134 ///
2135 /// Events will be queued indicating that there is STDOUT available that can
2136 /// be retrieved using this function.
2137 ///
2138 /// \param[out] buf
2139 /// A buffer that will receive any STDOUT bytes that are
2140 /// currently available.
2141 ///
2142 /// \param[in] buf_size
2143 /// The size in bytes for the buffer \a buf.
2144 ///
2145 /// \return
2146 /// The number of bytes written into \a buf. If this value is
2147 /// equal to \a buf_size, another call to this function should
2148 /// be made to retrieve more STDOUT data.
2149 virtual size_t GetSTDOUT(char *buf, size_t buf_size, Status &error);
2150
2151 /// Get any available STDERR.
2152 ///
2153 /// Calling this method is a valid operation only if all of the following
2154 /// conditions are true: 1) The process was launched, and not attached to.
2155 /// 2) The process was not launched with eLaunchFlagDisableSTDIO. 3) The
2156 /// process was launched without supplying a valid file path
2157 /// for STDERR.
2158 ///
2159 /// Note that the implementation will probably need to start a read thread
2160 /// in the background to make sure that the pipe is drained and the STDERR
2161 /// buffered appropriately, to prevent the process from deadlocking trying
2162 /// to write to a full buffer.
2163 ///
2164 /// Events will be queued indicating that there is STDERR available that can
2165 /// be retrieved using this function.
2166 ///
2167 /// \param[in] buf
2168 /// A buffer that will receive any STDERR bytes that are
2169 /// currently available.
2170 ///
2171 /// \param[out] buf_size
2172 /// The size in bytes for the buffer \a buf.
2173 ///
2174 /// \return
2175 /// The number of bytes written into \a buf. If this value is
2176 /// equal to \a buf_size, another call to this function should
2177 /// be made to retrieve more STDERR data.
2178 virtual size_t GetSTDERR(char *buf, size_t buf_size, Status &error);
2179
2180 /// Puts data into this process's STDIN.
2181 ///
2182 /// Calling this method is a valid operation only if all of the following
2183 /// conditions are true: 1) The process was launched, and not attached to.
2184 /// 2) The process was not launched with eLaunchFlagDisableSTDIO. 3) The
2185 /// process was launched without supplying a valid file path
2186 /// for STDIN.
2187 ///
2188 /// \param[in] buf
2189 /// A buffer that contains the data to write to the process's STDIN.
2190 ///
2191 /// \param[in] buf_size
2192 /// The size in bytes for the buffer \a buf.
2193 ///
2194 /// \return
2195 /// The number of bytes written into \a buf. If this value is
2196 /// less than \a buf_size, another call to this function should
2197 /// be made to write the rest of the data.
2198 virtual size_t PutSTDIN(const char *buf, size_t buf_size, Status &error) {
2199 error = Status::FromErrorString("stdin unsupported");
2200 return 0;
2201 }
2202
2203 /// Get any available profile data.
2204 ///
2205 /// \param[out] buf
2206 /// A buffer that will receive any profile data bytes that are
2207 /// currently available.
2208 ///
2209 /// \param[out] buf_size
2210 /// The size in bytes for the buffer \a buf.
2211 ///
2212 /// \return
2213 /// The number of bytes written into \a buf. If this value is
2214 /// equal to \a buf_size, another call to this function should
2215 /// be made to retrieve more profile data.
2216 virtual size_t GetAsyncProfileData(char *buf, size_t buf_size, Status &error);
2217
2218 // Process Breakpoints
2219 size_t GetSoftwareBreakpointTrapOpcode(BreakpointSite *bp_site);
2220
2221 virtual Status EnableBreakpointSite(BreakpointSite *bp_site) {
2222 return Status::FromErrorStringWithFormatv(
2223 "error: {0} does not support enabling breakpoints", GetPluginName());
2224 }
2225
2226 virtual Status DisableBreakpointSite(BreakpointSite *bp_site) {
2227 return Status::FromErrorStringWithFormatv(
2228 "error: {0} does not support disabling breakpoints", GetPluginName());
2229 }
2230
2231 // This is implemented completely using the lldb::Process API. Subclasses
2232 // don't need to implement this function unless the standard flow of read
2233 // existing opcode, write breakpoint opcode, verify breakpoint opcode doesn't
2234 // work for a specific process plug-in.
2235 virtual Status EnableSoftwareBreakpoint(BreakpointSite *bp_site);
2236
2237 // This is implemented completely using the lldb::Process API. Subclasses
2238 // don't need to implement this function unless the standard flow of
2239 // restoring original opcode in memory and verifying the restored opcode
2240 // doesn't work for a specific process plug-in.
2241 virtual Status DisableSoftwareBreakpoint(BreakpointSite *bp_site);
2242
2243 StopPointSiteList<lldb_private::BreakpointSite> &GetBreakpointSiteList();
2244
2245 const StopPointSiteList<lldb_private::BreakpointSite> &
2246 GetBreakpointSiteList() const;
2247
2248 void DisableAllBreakpointSites();
2249
2250 Status ClearBreakpointSiteByID(lldb::user_id_t break_id);
2251
2252 lldb::break_id_t CreateBreakpointSite(const lldb::BreakpointLocationSP &owner,
2253 bool use_hardware);
2254
2255 Status DisableBreakpointSiteByID(lldb::user_id_t break_id);
2256
2257 Status EnableBreakpointSiteByID(lldb::user_id_t break_id);
2258
2259 // BreakpointLocations use RemoveConstituentFromBreakpointSite to remove
2260 // themselves from the constituent's list of this breakpoint sites.
2261 void RemoveConstituentFromBreakpointSite(lldb::user_id_t site_id,
2262 lldb::user_id_t constituent_id,
2263 lldb::BreakpointSiteSP &bp_site_sp);
2264
2265 // Process Watchpoints (optional)
2266 virtual Status EnableWatchpoint(lldb::WatchpointSP wp_sp, bool notify = true);
2267
2268 virtual Status DisableWatchpoint(lldb::WatchpointSP wp_sp,
2269 bool notify = true);
2270
2271 // Thread Queries
2272
2273 /// Update the thread list.
2274 ///
2275 /// This method performs some general clean up before invoking
2276 /// \a DoUpdateThreadList, which should be implemented by each
2277 /// process plugin.
2278 ///
2279 /// \return
2280 /// \b true if the new thread list could be generated, \b false otherwise.
2281 bool UpdateThreadList(ThreadList &old_thread_list,
2282 ThreadList &new_thread_list);
2283
2284 void UpdateThreadListIfNeeded();
2285
2286 ThreadList &GetThreadList() { return m_thread_list; }
2287
2288 StopPointSiteList<lldb_private::WatchpointResource> &
2289 GetWatchpointResourceList() {
2290 return m_watchpoint_resource_list;
2291 }
2292
2293 // When ExtendedBacktraces are requested, the HistoryThreads that are created
2294 // need an owner -- they're saved here in the Process. The threads in this
2295 // list are not iterated over - driver programs need to request the extended
2296 // backtrace calls starting from a root concrete thread one by one.
2297 ThreadList &GetExtendedThreadList() { return m_extended_thread_list; }
2298
2299 ThreadList::ThreadIterable Threads() { return m_thread_list.Threads(); }
2300
2301 uint32_t GetNextThreadIndexID(uint64_t thread_id);
2302
2303 lldb::ThreadSP CreateOSPluginThread(lldb::tid_t tid, lldb::addr_t context);
2304
2305 // Returns true if an index id has been assigned to a thread.
2306 bool HasAssignedIndexIDToThread(uint64_t sb_thread_id);
2307
2308 // Given a thread_id, it will assign a more reasonable index id for display
2309 // to the user. If the thread_id has previously been assigned, the same index
2310 // id will be used.
2311 uint32_t AssignIndexIDToThread(uint64_t thread_id);
2312
2313 // Queue Queries
2314
2315 virtual void UpdateQueueListIfNeeded();
2316
2317 QueueList &GetQueueList() {
2318 UpdateQueueListIfNeeded();
2319 return m_queue_list;
2320 }
2321
2322 QueueList::QueueIterable Queues() {
2323 UpdateQueueListIfNeeded();
2324 return m_queue_list.Queues();
2325 }
2326
2327 // Event Handling
2328 lldb::StateType GetNextEvent(lldb::EventSP &event_sp);
2329
2330 // Returns the process state when it is stopped. If specified, event_sp_ptr
2331 // is set to the event which triggered the stop. If wait_always = false, and
2332 // the process is already stopped, this function returns immediately. If the
2333 // process is hijacked and use_run_lock is true (the default), then this
2334 // function releases the run lock after the stop. Setting use_run_lock to
2335 // false will avoid this behavior.
2336 // If we are waiting to stop that will return control to the user,
2337 // then we also want to run SelectMostRelevantFrame, which is controlled
2338 // by "select_most_relevant".
2339 lldb::StateType
2340 WaitForProcessToStop(const Timeout<std::micro> &timeout,
2341 lldb::EventSP *event_sp_ptr = nullptr,
2342 bool wait_always = true,
2343 lldb::ListenerSP hijack_listener = lldb::ListenerSP(),
2344 Stream *stream = nullptr, bool use_run_lock = true,
2345 SelectMostRelevant select_most_relevant =
2346 DoNoSelectMostRelevantFrame);
2347
2348 uint32_t GetIOHandlerID() const { return m_iohandler_sync.GetValue(); }
2349
2350 /// Waits for the process state to be running within a given msec timeout.
2351 ///
2352 /// The main purpose of this is to implement an interlock waiting for
2353 /// HandlePrivateEvent to push an IOHandler.
2354 ///
2355 /// \param[in] timeout
2356 /// The maximum time length to wait for the process to transition to the
2357 /// eStateRunning state.
2358 void SyncIOHandler(uint32_t iohandler_id, const Timeout<std::micro> &timeout);
2359
2360 lldb::StateType GetStateChangedEvents(
2361 lldb::EventSP &event_sp, const Timeout<std::micro> &timeout,
2362 lldb::ListenerSP
2363 hijack_listener); // Pass an empty ListenerSP to use builtin listener
2364
2365 /// Centralize the code that handles and prints descriptions for process
2366 /// state changes.
2367 ///
2368 /// \param[in] event_sp
2369 /// The process state changed event
2370 ///
2371 /// \param[in] stream
2372 /// The output stream to get the state change description
2373 ///
2374 /// \param[in,out] pop_process_io_handler
2375 /// If this value comes in set to \b true, then pop the Process IOHandler
2376 /// if needed.
2377 /// Else this variable will be set to \b true or \b false to indicate if
2378 /// the process
2379 /// needs to have its process IOHandler popped.
2380 ///
2381 /// \return
2382 /// \b true if the event describes a process state changed event, \b false
2383 /// otherwise.
2384 static bool
2385 HandleProcessStateChangedEvent(const lldb::EventSP &event_sp, Stream *stream,
2386 SelectMostRelevant select_most_relevant,
2387 bool &pop_process_io_handler);
2388
2389 Event *PeekAtStateChangedEvents();
2390
2391 class ProcessEventHijacker {
2392 public:
2393 ProcessEventHijacker(Process &process, lldb::ListenerSP listener_sp)
2394 : m_process(process) {
2395 m_process.HijackProcessEvents(std::move(listener_sp));
2396 }
2397
2398 ~ProcessEventHijacker() { m_process.RestoreProcessEvents(); }
2399
2400 private:
2401 Process &m_process;
2402 };
2403
2404 friend class ProcessEventHijacker;
2405 friend class ProcessProperties;
2406 /// If you need to ensure that you and only you will hear about some public
2407 /// event, then make a new listener, set to listen to process events, and
2408 /// then call this with that listener. Then you will have to wait on that
2409 /// listener explicitly for events (rather than using the GetNextEvent &
2410 /// WaitFor* calls above. Be sure to call RestoreProcessEvents when you are
2411 /// done.
2412 ///
2413 /// \param[in] listener_sp
2414 /// This is the new listener to whom all process events will be delivered.
2415 ///
2416 /// \return
2417 /// Returns \b true if the new listener could be installed,
2418 /// \b false otherwise.
2419 bool HijackProcessEvents(lldb::ListenerSP listener_sp);
2420
2421 /// Restores the process event broadcasting to its normal state.
2422 ///
2423 void RestoreProcessEvents();
2424
2425 bool StateChangedIsHijackedForSynchronousResume();
2426
2427 bool StateChangedIsExternallyHijacked();
2428
2429 const lldb::ABISP &GetABI();
2430
2431 OperatingSystem *GetOperatingSystem() { return m_os_up.get(); }
2432
2433 std::vector<LanguageRuntime *> GetLanguageRuntimes();
2434
2435 LanguageRuntime *GetLanguageRuntime(lldb::LanguageType language);
2436
2437 bool IsPossibleDynamicValue(ValueObject &in_value);
2438
2439 bool IsRunning() const;
2440
2441 DynamicCheckerFunctions *GetDynamicCheckers() {
2442 return m_dynamic_checkers_up.get();
2443 }
2444
2445 void SetDynamicCheckers(DynamicCheckerFunctions *dynamic_checkers);
2446
2447/// Prune ThreadPlanStacks for unreported threads.
2448///
2449/// \param[in] tid
2450/// The tid whose Plan Stack we are seeking to prune.
2451///
2452/// \return
2453/// \b true if the TID is found or \b false if not.
2454bool PruneThreadPlansForTID(lldb::tid_t tid);
2455
2456/// Prune ThreadPlanStacks for all unreported threads.
2457void PruneThreadPlans();
2458
2459 /// Find the thread plan stack associated with thread with \a tid.
2460 ///
2461 /// \param[in] tid
2462 /// The tid whose Plan Stack we are seeking.
2463 ///
2464 /// \return
2465 /// Returns a ThreadPlan if the TID is found or nullptr if not.
2466 ThreadPlanStack *FindThreadPlans(lldb::tid_t tid);
2467
2468 /// Dump the thread plans associated with thread with \a tid.
2469 ///
2470 /// \param[in,out] strm
2471 /// The stream to which to dump the output
2472 ///
2473 /// \param[in] tid
2474 /// The tid whose Plan Stack we are dumping
2475 ///
2476 /// \param[in] desc_level
2477 /// How much detail to dump
2478 ///
2479 /// \param[in] internal
2480 /// If \b true dump all plans, if false only user initiated plans
2481 ///
2482 /// \param[in] condense_trivial
2483 /// If true, only dump a header if the plan stack is just the base plan.
2484 ///
2485 /// \param[in] skip_unreported_plans
2486 /// If true, only dump a plan if it is currently backed by an
2487 /// lldb_private::Thread *.
2488 ///
2489 /// \return
2490 /// Returns \b true if TID was found, \b false otherwise
2491 bool DumpThreadPlansForTID(Stream &strm, lldb::tid_t tid,
2492 lldb::DescriptionLevel desc_level, bool internal,
2493 bool condense_trivial, bool skip_unreported_plans);
2494
2495 /// Dump all the thread plans for this process.
2496 ///
2497 /// \param[in,out] strm
2498 /// The stream to which to dump the output
2499 ///
2500 /// \param[in] desc_level
2501 /// How much detail to dump
2502 ///
2503 /// \param[in] internal
2504 /// If \b true dump all plans, if false only user initiated plans
2505 ///
2506 /// \param[in] condense_trivial
2507 /// If true, only dump a header if the plan stack is just the base plan.
2508 ///
2509 /// \param[in] skip_unreported_plans
2510 /// If true, skip printing all thread plan stacks that don't currently
2511 /// have a backing lldb_private::Thread *.
2512 void DumpThreadPlans(Stream &strm, lldb::DescriptionLevel desc_level,
2513 bool internal, bool condense_trivial,
2514 bool skip_unreported_plans);
2515
2516 /// Call this to set the lldb in the mode where it breaks on new thread
2517 /// creations, and then auto-restarts. This is useful when you are trying
2518 /// to run only one thread, but either that thread or the kernel is creating
2519 /// new threads in the process. If you stop when the thread is created, you
2520 /// can immediately suspend it, and keep executing only the one thread you
2521 /// intend.
2522 ///
2523 /// \return
2524 /// Returns \b true if we were able to start up the notification
2525 /// \b false otherwise.
2526 virtual bool StartNoticingNewThreads() { return true; }
2527
2528 /// Call this to turn off the stop & notice new threads mode.
2529 ///
2530 /// \return
2531 /// Returns \b true if we were able to start up the notification
2532 /// \b false otherwise.
2533 virtual bool StopNoticingNewThreads() { return true; }
2534
2535 void SetRunningUserExpression(bool on);
2536 void SetRunningUtilityFunction(bool on);
2537
2538 // lldb::ExecutionContextScope pure virtual functions
2539 lldb::TargetSP CalculateTarget() override;
2540
2541 lldb::ProcessSP CalculateProcess() override { return shared_from_this(); }
2542
2543 lldb::ThreadSP CalculateThread() override { return lldb::ThreadSP(); }
2544
2545 lldb::StackFrameSP CalculateStackFrame() override {
2546 return lldb::StackFrameSP();
2547 }
2548
2549 void CalculateExecutionContext(ExecutionContext &exe_ctx) override;
2550
2551#ifdef _WIN32
2552 /// Associates a ConPTY read and write HANDLEs with the process' STDIO
2553 /// handling and configures an asynchronous reading of that ConPTY's stdout
2554 /// HANDLE.
2555 ///
2556 /// This method installs a ConnectionGenericFile for the passed ConPTY and
2557 /// starts a dedicated read thread. If the read thread starts successfully,
2558 /// the method also ensures that an IOHandlerProcessSTDIOWindows is created to
2559 /// manage user input to the process.
2560 ///
2561 /// When data is successfully read from the ConPTY, it is stored in
2562 /// m_stdout_data. There is no differentiation between stdout and stderr.
2563 ///
2564 /// \see lldb_private::Process::STDIOReadThreadBytesReceived()
2565 /// \see lldb_private::IOHandlerProcessSTDIOWindows
2566 /// \see lldb_private::PseudoConsole
2567 virtual void SetPseudoConsoleHandle() {};
2568#endif
2569
2570 /// Associates a file descriptor with the process' STDIO handling
2571 /// and configures an asynchronous reading of that descriptor.
2572 ///
2573 /// This method installs a ConnectionFileDescriptor for the passed file
2574 /// descriptor and starts a dedicated read thread. If the read thread starts
2575 /// successfully, the method also ensures that an IOHandlerProcessSTDIO is
2576 /// created to manage user input to the process.
2577 ///
2578 /// The descriptor's ownership is transferred to the underlying
2579 /// ConnectionFileDescriptor.
2580 ///
2581 /// When data is successfully read from the file descriptor, it is stored in
2582 /// m_stdout_data. There is no differentiation between stdout and stderr.
2583 ///
2584 /// \param[in] fd
2585 /// The file descriptor to use for process STDIO communication. It's
2586 /// assumed to be valid and will be managed by the newly created
2587 /// connection.
2588 ///
2589 /// \see lldb_private::Process::STDIOReadThreadBytesReceived()
2590 /// \see lldb_private::IOHandlerProcessSTDIO
2591 /// \see lldb_private::ConnectionFileDescriptor
2592 void SetSTDIOFileDescriptor(int file_descriptor);
2593
2594 // Add a permanent region of memory that should never be read or written to.
2595 // This can be used to ensure that memory reads or writes to certain areas of
2596 // memory never end up being sent to the DoReadMemory or DoWriteMemory
2597 // functions which can improve performance.
2598 void AddInvalidMemoryRegion(const LoadRange &region);
2599
2600 // Remove a permanent region of memory that should never be read or written
2601 // to that was previously added with AddInvalidMemoryRegion.
2602 bool RemoveInvalidMemoryRange(const LoadRange &region);
2603
2604 // If the setup code of a thread plan needs to do work that might involve
2605 // calling a function in the target, it should not do that work directly in
2606 // one of the thread plan functions (DidPush/WillResume) because such work
2607 // needs to be handled carefully. Instead, put that work in a
2608 // PreResumeAction callback, and register it with the process. It will get
2609 // done before the actual "DoResume" gets called.
2610
2611 typedef bool(PreResumeActionCallback)(void *);
2612
2613 void AddPreResumeAction(PreResumeActionCallback callback, void *baton);
2614
2615 bool RunPreResumeActions();
2616
2617 void ClearPreResumeActions();
2618
2619 void ClearPreResumeAction(PreResumeActionCallback callback, void *baton);
2620
2621 ProcessRunLock &GetRunLock();
2622
2623 bool CurrentThreadIsPrivateStateThread();
2624
2625 bool CurrentThreadPosesAsPrivateStateThread();
2626
2627 virtual Status SendEventData(const char *data) {
2628 return Status::FromErrorString(
2629 "Sending an event is not supported for this process.");
2630 }
2631
2632 lldb::ThreadCollectionSP GetHistoryThreads(lldb::addr_t addr);
2633
2634 lldb::InstrumentationRuntimeSP
2635 GetInstrumentationRuntime(lldb::InstrumentationRuntimeType type);
2636
2637 /// Try to fetch the module specification for a module with the given file
2638 /// name and architecture. Process sub-classes have to override this method
2639 /// if they support platforms where the Platform object can't get the module
2640 /// spec for all module.
2641 ///
2642 /// \param[in] module_file_spec
2643 /// The file name of the module to get specification for.
2644 ///
2645 /// \param[in] arch
2646 /// The architecture of the module to get specification for.
2647 ///
2648 /// \param[out] module_spec
2649 /// The fetched module specification if the return value is
2650 /// \b true, unchanged otherwise.
2651 ///
2652 /// \return
2653 /// Returns \b true if the module spec fetched successfully,
2654 /// \b false otherwise.
2655 virtual bool GetModuleSpec(const FileSpec &module_file_spec,
2656 const ArchSpec &arch, ModuleSpec &module_spec);
2657
2658 virtual void PrefetchModuleSpecs(llvm::ArrayRef<FileSpec> module_file_specs,
2659 const llvm::Triple &triple) {}
2660
2661 /// Try to find the load address of a file.
2662 /// The load address is defined as the address of the first memory region
2663 /// what contains data mapped from the specified file.
2664 ///
2665 /// \param[in] file
2666 /// The name of the file whose load address we are looking for
2667 ///
2668 /// \param[out] is_loaded
2669 /// \b True if the file is loaded into the memory and false
2670 /// otherwise.
2671 ///
2672 /// \param[out] load_addr
2673 /// The load address of the file if it is loaded into the
2674 /// processes address space, LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS otherwise.
2675 virtual Status GetFileLoadAddress(const FileSpec &file, bool &is_loaded,
2676 lldb::addr_t &load_addr) {
2677 return Status::FromErrorString("Not supported");
2678 }
2679
2680 /// Fetch process defined metadata.
2681 ///
2682 /// \return
2683 /// A StructuredDataSP object which, if non-empty, will contain the
2684 /// information related to the process.
2685 virtual StructuredData::DictionarySP GetMetadata() { return nullptr; }
2686
2687 /// Fetch extended crash information held by the process. This will never be
2688 /// an empty shared pointer, it will always have a dict, though it may be
2689 /// empty.
2690 StructuredData::DictionarySP GetExtendedCrashInfoDict() {
2691 assert(m_crash_info_dict_sp && "We always have a valid dictionary");
2692 return m_crash_info_dict_sp;
2693 }
2694
2695 void ResetExtendedCrashInfoDict() {
2696 // StructuredData::Dictionary is add only, so we have to make a new one:
2697 m_crash_info_dict_sp = std::make_shared<StructuredData::Dictionary>();
2698 }
2699
2700 size_t AddImageToken(lldb::addr_t image_ptr);
2701
2702 lldb::addr_t GetImagePtrFromToken(size_t token) const;
2703
2704 void ResetImageToken(size_t token);
2705
2706 /// Find the next branch instruction to set a breakpoint on
2707 ///
2708 /// When instruction stepping through a source line, instead of stepping
2709 /// through each instruction, we can put a breakpoint on the next branch
2710 /// instruction (within the range of instructions we are stepping through)
2711 /// and continue the process to there, yielding significant performance
2712 /// benefits over instruction stepping.
2713 ///
2714 /// \param[in] default_stop_addr
2715 /// The address of the instruction where lldb would put a
2716 /// breakpoint normally.
2717 ///
2718 /// \param[in] range_bounds
2719 /// The range which the breakpoint must be contained within.
2720 /// Typically a source line.
2721 ///
2722 /// \return
2723 /// The address of the next branch instruction, or the end of
2724 /// the range provided in range_bounds. If there are any
2725 /// problems with the disassembly or getting the instructions,
2726 /// the original default_stop_addr will be returned.
2727 Address AdvanceAddressToNextBranchInstruction(Address default_stop_addr,
2728 AddressRange range_bounds);
2729
2730 /// Configure asynchronous structured data feature.
2731 ///
2732 /// Each Process type that supports using an asynchronous StructuredData
2733 /// feature should implement this to enable/disable/configure the feature.
2734 /// The default implementation here will always return an error indiciating
2735 /// the feature is unsupported.
2736 ///
2737 /// StructuredDataPlugin implementations will call this to configure a
2738 /// feature that has been reported as being supported.
2739 ///
2740 /// \param[in] type_name
2741 /// The StructuredData type name as previously discovered by
2742 /// the Process-derived instance.
2743 ///
2744 /// \param[in] config_sp
2745 /// Configuration data for the feature being enabled. This config
2746 /// data, which may be null, will be passed along to the feature
2747 /// to process. The feature will dictate whether this is a dictionary,
2748 /// an array or some other object. If the feature needs to be
2749 /// set up properly before it can be enabled, then the config should
2750 /// also take an enable/disable flag.
2751 ///
2752 /// \return
2753 /// Returns the result of attempting to configure the feature.
2754 virtual Status
2755 ConfigureStructuredData(llvm::StringRef type_name,
2756 const StructuredData::ObjectSP &config_sp);
2757
2758 /// Broadcasts the given structured data object from the given plugin.
2759 ///
2760 /// StructuredDataPlugin instances can use this to optionally broadcast any
2761 /// of their data if they want to make it available for clients. The data
2762 /// will come in on the structured data event bit
2763 /// (eBroadcastBitStructuredData).
2764 ///
2765 /// \param[in] object_sp
2766 /// The structured data object to broadcast.
2767 ///
2768 /// \param[in] plugin_sp
2769 /// The plugin that will be reported in the event's plugin
2770 /// parameter.
2771 void BroadcastStructuredData(const StructuredData::ObjectSP &object_sp,
2772 const lldb::StructuredDataPluginSP &plugin_sp);
2773
2774 /// Returns the StructuredDataPlugin associated with a given type name, if
2775 /// there is one.
2776 ///
2777 /// There will only be a plugin for a given StructuredDataType if the
2778 /// debugged process monitor claims that the feature is supported. This is
2779 /// one way to tell whether a feature is available.
2780 ///
2781 /// \return
2782 /// The plugin if one is available for the specified feature;
2783 /// otherwise, returns an empty shared pointer.
2784 lldb::StructuredDataPluginSP
2785 GetStructuredDataPlugin(llvm::StringRef type_name) const;
2786
2787 virtual void *GetImplementation() { return nullptr; }
2788
2789 virtual void ForceScriptedState(lldb::StateType state) {}
2790
2791 SourceManager::SourceFileCache &GetSourceFileCache() {
2792 return m_source_file_cache;
2793 }
2794
2795 /// Find a pattern within a memory region.
2796 ///
2797 /// This function searches for a pattern represented by the provided buffer
2798 /// within the memory range specified by the low and high addresses. It uses
2799 /// a bad character heuristic to optimize the search process.
2800 ///
2801 /// \param[in] low The starting address of the memory region to be searched.
2802 /// (inclusive)
2803 ///
2804 /// \param[in] high The ending address of the memory region to be searched.
2805 /// (exclusive)
2806 ///
2807 /// \param[in] buf A pointer to the buffer containing the pattern to be
2808 /// searched.
2809 ///
2810 /// \param[in] buffer_size The size of the buffer in bytes.
2811 ///
2812 /// \return The address where the pattern was found or LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS if
2813 /// not found.
2814 lldb::addr_t FindInMemory(lldb::addr_t low, lldb::addr_t high,
2815 const uint8_t *buf, size_t size);
2816
2817 AddressRanges FindRangesInMemory(const uint8_t *buf, uint64_t size,
2818 const AddressRanges &ranges,
2819 size_t alignment, size_t max_matches,
2820 Status &error);
2821
2822 lldb::addr_t FindInMemory(const uint8_t *buf, uint64_t size,
2823 const AddressRange &range, size_t alignment,
2824 Status &error);
2825
2826 /// Get the base run direction for the process.
2827 /// The base direction is the direction the process will execute in
2828 /// (forward or backward) if no thread plan overrides the direction.
2829 lldb::RunDirection GetBaseDirection() const { return m_base_direction; }
2830 /// Set the base run direction for the process.
2831 /// As a side-effect, if this changes the base direction, then we
2832 /// discard all non-base thread plans to ensure that when execution resumes
2833 /// we definitely execute in the requested direction.
2834 /// FIXME: this is overkill. In some situations ensuring the latter
2835 /// would not require discarding all non-base thread plans.
2836 void SetBaseDirection(lldb::RunDirection direction);
2837
2838protected:
2839 friend class Trace;
2840
2841 /// Construct with a shared pointer to a target, and the Process listener.
2842 /// Uses the Host UnixSignalsSP by default.
2843 Process(lldb::TargetSP target_sp, lldb::ListenerSP listener_sp);
2844
2845 /// Construct with a shared pointer to a target, the Process listener, and
2846 /// the appropriate UnixSignalsSP for the process.
2847 Process(lldb::TargetSP target_sp, lldb::ListenerSP listener_sp,
2848 const lldb::UnixSignalsSP &unix_signals_sp);
2849
2850 /// Get the processor tracing type supported for this process.
2851 /// Responses might be different depending on the architecture and
2852 /// capabilities of the underlying OS.
2853 ///
2854 /// \return
2855 /// The supported trace type or an \a llvm::Error if tracing is
2856 /// not supported for the inferior.
2857 virtual llvm::Expected<TraceSupportedResponse> TraceSupported();
2858
2859 /// Start tracing a process or its threads.
2860 ///
2861 /// \param[in] request
2862 /// JSON object with the information necessary to start tracing. In the
2863 /// case of gdb-remote processes, this JSON object should conform to the
2864 /// jLLDBTraceStart packet.
2865 ///
2866 /// \return
2867 /// \a llvm::Error::success if the operation was successful, or
2868 /// \a llvm::Error otherwise.
2869 virtual llvm::Error TraceStart(const llvm::json::Value &request) {
2870 return llvm::make_error<UnimplementedError>();
2871 }
2872
2873 /// Stop tracing a live process or its threads.
2874 ///
2875 /// \param[in] request
2876 /// The information determining which threads or process to stop tracing.
2877 ///
2878 /// \return
2879 /// \a llvm::Error::success if the operation was successful, or
2880 /// \a llvm::Error otherwise.
2881 virtual llvm::Error TraceStop(const TraceStopRequest &request) {
2882 return llvm::make_error<UnimplementedError>();
2883 }
2884
2885 /// Get the current tracing state of the process and its threads.
2886 ///
2887 /// \param[in] type
2888 /// Tracing technology type to consider.
2889 ///
2890 /// \return
2891 /// A JSON object string with custom data depending on the trace
2892 /// technology, or an \a llvm::Error in case of errors.
2893 virtual llvm::Expected<std::string> TraceGetState(llvm::StringRef type) {
2894 return llvm::make_error<UnimplementedError>();
2895 }
2896
2897 /// Get binary data given a trace technology and a data identifier.
2898 ///
2899 /// \param[in] request
2900 /// Object with the params of the requested data.
2901 ///
2902 /// \return
2903 /// A vector of bytes with the requested data, or an \a llvm::Error in
2904 /// case of failures.
2905 virtual llvm::Expected<std::vector<uint8_t>>
2906 TraceGetBinaryData(const TraceGetBinaryDataRequest &request) {
2907 return llvm::make_error<UnimplementedError>();
2908 }
2909
2910 // This calls a function of the form "void * (*)(void)".
2911 bool CallVoidArgVoidPtrReturn(const Address *address,
2912 lldb::addr_t &returned_func,
2913 bool trap_exceptions = false);
2914
2915 /// Update the thread list following process plug-in's specific logic.
2916 ///
2917 /// This method should only be invoked by \a UpdateThreadList.
2918 ///
2919 /// \return
2920 /// \b true if the new thread list could be generated, \b false otherwise.
2921 virtual bool DoUpdateThreadList(ThreadList &old_thread_list,
2922 ThreadList &new_thread_list) = 0;
2923
2924 /// Actually do the reading of memory from a process.
2925 ///
2926 /// Subclasses must override this function and can return fewer bytes than
2927 /// requested when memory requests are too large. This class will break up
2928 /// the memory requests and keep advancing the arguments along as needed.
2929 ///
2930 /// \param[in] vm_addr
2931 /// A virtual load address that indicates where to start reading
2932 /// memory from.
2933 ///
2934 /// \param[in] size
2935 /// The number of bytes to read.
2936 ///
2937 /// \param[out] buf
2938 /// A byte buffer that is at least \a size bytes long that
2939 /// will receive the memory bytes.
2940 ///
2941 /// \param[out] error
2942 /// An error that indicates the success or failure of this
2943 /// operation. If error indicates success (error.Success()),
2944 /// then the value returned can be trusted, otherwise zero
2945 /// will be returned.
2946 ///
2947 /// \return
2948 /// The number of bytes that were actually read into \a buf.
2949 /// Zero is returned in the case of an error.
2950 virtual size_t DoReadMemory(lldb::addr_t vm_addr, void *buf, size_t size,
2951 Status &error) = 0;
2952
2953 virtual void DoFindInMemory(lldb::addr_t start_addr, lldb::addr_t end_addr,
2954 const uint8_t *buf, size_t size,
2955 AddressRanges &matches, size_t alignment,
2956 size_t max_matches);
2957
2958 /// DoGetMemoryRegionInfo is called by GetMemoryRegionInfo after it has
2959 /// removed non address bits from load_addr. Override this method in
2960 /// subclasses of Process.
2961 ///
2962 /// See GetMemoryRegionInfo for details of the logic.
2963 ///
2964 /// \param[in] load_addr
2965 /// The load address to query the range_info for. (non address bits
2966 /// removed)
2967 ///
2968 /// \param[out] range_info
2969 /// An range_info value containing the details of the range.
2970 ///
2971 /// \return
2972 /// An error value.
2973 virtual Status DoGetMemoryRegionInfo(lldb::addr_t load_addr,
2974 MemoryRegionInfo &range_info) {
2975 return Status::FromErrorString(
2976 "Process::DoGetMemoryRegionInfo() not supported");
2977 }
2978
2979 /// Provide an override value in the subclass for lldb's
2980 /// CPU-based logic for whether watchpoint exceptions are
2981 /// received before or after an instruction executes.
2982 ///
2983 /// If a Process subclass needs to override this architecture-based
2984 /// result, it may do so by overriding this method.
2985 ///
2986 /// \return
2987 /// No boolean returned means there is no override of the
2988 /// default architecture-based behavior.
2989 /// true is returned for targets where watchpoints are reported
2990 /// after the instruction has completed.
2991 /// false is returned for targets where watchpoints are reported
2992 /// before the instruction executes.
2993 virtual std::optional<bool> DoGetWatchpointReportedAfter() {
2994 return std::nullopt;
2995 }
2996
2997 /// Handle thread specific async interrupt and return the original thread
2998 /// that requested the async interrupt. It can be null if original thread
2999 /// has exited.
3000 ///
3001 /// \param[in] description
3002 /// Returns the stop reason description of the async interrupt.
3003 virtual lldb::ThreadSP
3004 HandleThreadAsyncInterrupt(uint8_t signo, const std::string &description) {
3005 return lldb::ThreadSP();
3006 }
3007
3008 /// The "private" side of resuming a process. This doesn't alter the state
3009 /// of m_run_lock, but just causes the process to resume.
3010 ///
3011 /// \return
3012 /// An Status object describing the success or failure of the resume.
3013 Status PrivateResume();
3014
3015 // Called internally
3016 void CompleteAttach();
3017
3018 // NextEventAction provides a way to register an action on the next event
3019 // that is delivered to this process. There is currently only one next event
3020 // action allowed in the process at one time. If a new "NextEventAction" is
3021 // added while one is already present, the old action will be discarded (with
3022 // HandleBeingUnshipped called after it is discarded.)
3023 //
3024 // If you want to resume the process as a result of a resume action, call
3025 // RequestResume, don't call Resume directly.
3026 class NextEventAction {
3027 public:
3028 enum EventActionResult {
3029 eEventActionSuccess,
3030 eEventActionRetry,
3031 eEventActionExit
3032 };
3033
3034 NextEventAction(Process *process) : m_process(process) {}
3035
3036 virtual ~NextEventAction() = default;
3037
3038 virtual EventActionResult PerformAction(lldb::EventSP &event_sp) = 0;
3039 virtual void HandleBeingUnshipped() {}
3040 virtual EventActionResult HandleBeingInterrupted() = 0;
3041 virtual const char *GetExitString() = 0;
3042 void RequestResume() { m_process->m_resume_requested = true; }
3043
3044 protected:
3045 Process *m_process;
3046 };
3047
3048 void SetNextEventAction(Process::NextEventAction *next_event_action) {
3049 if (m_next_event_action_up)
3050 m_next_event_action_up->HandleBeingUnshipped();
3051
3052 m_next_event_action_up.reset(next_event_action);
3053 }
3054
3055 // This is the completer for Attaching:
3056 class AttachCompletionHandler : public NextEventAction {
3057 public:
3058 AttachCompletionHandler(Process *process, uint32_t exec_count);
3059
3060 ~AttachCompletionHandler() override = default;
3061
3062 EventActionResult PerformAction(lldb::EventSP &event_sp) override;
3063 EventActionResult HandleBeingInterrupted() override;
3064 const char *GetExitString() override;
3065
3066 private:
3067 uint32_t m_exec_count;
3068 std::string m_exit_string;
3069 };
3070
3071 bool PrivateStateThreadIsRunning() const {
3072 if (!m_current_private_state_thread ||
3073 !m_current_private_state_thread->IsRunning())
3074 return false;
3075
3076 lldb::StateType state = m_current_private_state_thread->GetPrivateState();
3077 return state != lldb::eStateInvalid && state != lldb::eStateDetached &&
3078 state != lldb::eStateExited;
3079 }
3080
3081 void ForceNextEventDelivery() { m_force_next_event_delivery = true; }
3082
3083 /// Loads any plugins associated with asynchronous structured data and maps
3084 /// the relevant supported type name to the plugin.
3085 ///
3086 /// Processes can receive asynchronous structured data from the process
3087 /// monitor. This method will load and map any structured data plugins that
3088 /// support the given set of supported type names. Later, if any of these
3089 /// features are enabled, the process monitor is free to generate
3090 /// asynchronous structured data. The data must come in as a single \b
3091 /// StructuredData::Dictionary. That dictionary must have a string field
3092 /// named 'type', with a value that equals the relevant type name string
3093 /// (one of the values in \b supported_type_names).
3094 ///
3095 /// \param[in] supported_type_names
3096 /// An array of zero or more type names. Each must be unique.
3097 /// For each entry in the list, a StructuredDataPlugin will be
3098 /// searched for that supports the structured data type name.
3099 void MapSupportedStructuredDataPlugins(
3100 const StructuredData::Array &supported_type_names);
3101
3102 /// Route the incoming structured data dictionary to the right plugin.
3103 ///
3104 /// The incoming structured data must be a dictionary, and it must have a
3105 /// key named 'type' that stores a string value. The string value must be
3106 /// the name of the structured data feature that knows how to handle it.
3107 ///
3108 /// \param[in] object_sp
3109 /// When non-null and pointing to a dictionary, the 'type'
3110 /// key's string value is used to look up the plugin that
3111 /// was registered for that structured data type. It then
3112 /// calls the following method on the StructuredDataPlugin
3113 /// instance:
3114 ///
3115 /// virtual void
3116 /// HandleArrivalOfStructuredData(Process &process,
3117 /// llvm::StringRef type_name,
3118 /// const StructuredData::ObjectSP
3119 /// &object_sp)
3120 ///
3121 /// \return
3122 /// True if the structured data was routed to a plugin; otherwise,
3123 /// false.
3124 bool RouteAsyncStructuredData(const StructuredData::ObjectSP object_sp);
3125
3126 /// Check whether the process supports memory tagging.
3127 ///
3128 /// \return
3129 /// true if the process supports memory tagging,
3130 /// false otherwise.
3131 virtual bool SupportsMemoryTagging() { return false; }
3132
3133 /// Does the final operation to read memory tags. E.g. sending a GDB packet.
3134 /// It assumes that ReadMemoryTags has checked that memory tagging is enabled
3135 /// and has expanded the memory range as needed.
3136 ///
3137 /// \param[in] addr
3138 /// Start of address range to read memory tags for.
3139 ///
3140 /// \param[in] len
3141 /// Length of the memory range to read tags for (in bytes).
3142 ///
3143 /// \param[in] type
3144 /// Type of tags to read (get this from a MemoryTagManager)
3145 ///
3146 /// \return
3147 /// The packed tag data received from the remote or an error
3148 /// if the read failed.
3149 virtual llvm::Expected<std::vector<uint8_t>>
3150 DoReadMemoryTags(lldb::addr_t addr, size_t len, int32_t type) {
3151 return llvm::createStringError(
3152 llvm::inconvertibleErrorCode(),
3153 llvm::formatv("{0} does not support reading memory tags",
3154 GetPluginName()));
3155 }
3156
3157 /// Does the final operation to write memory tags. E.g. sending a GDB packet.
3158 /// It assumes that WriteMemoryTags has checked that memory tagging is enabled
3159 /// and has packed the tag data.
3160 ///
3161 /// \param[in] addr
3162 /// Start of address range to write memory tags for.
3163 ///
3164 /// \param[in] len
3165 /// Length of the memory range to write tags for (in bytes).
3166 ///
3167 /// \param[in] type
3168 /// Type of tags to read (get this from a MemoryTagManager)
3169 ///
3170 /// \param[in] tags
3171 /// Packed tags to be written.
3172 ///
3173 /// \return
3174 /// Status telling you whether the write succeeded.
3175 virtual Status DoWriteMemoryTags(lldb::addr_t addr, size_t len, int32_t type,
3176 const std::vector<uint8_t> &tags) {
3177 return Status::FromErrorStringWithFormatv(
3178 "{0} does not support writing memory tags", GetPluginName());
3179 }
3180
3181 // Type definitions
3182 typedef std::map<lldb::LanguageType, lldb::LanguageRuntimeSP>
3183 LanguageRuntimeCollection;
3184
3185 struct PreResumeCallbackAndBaton {
3186 bool (*callback)(void *);
3187 void *baton;
3188 PreResumeCallbackAndBaton(PreResumeActionCallback in_callback,
3189 void *in_baton)
3190 : callback(in_callback), baton(in_baton) {}
3191 bool operator== (const PreResumeCallbackAndBaton &rhs) {
3192 return callback == rhs.callback && baton == rhs.baton;
3193 }
3194 };
3195
3196 /// The PrivateStateThread struct gathers all the bits of state needed to
3197 /// manage handling Process events, from receiving them on the Private State
3198 /// to signaling when process events are broadcase publicly, to determining
3199 /// when various actors can act on the process. It also holds the current
3200 /// private state thread.
3201 /// These need to be swappable as a group to manage the temporary modal
3202 /// private state thread that we spin up when we need to run an expression on
3203 /// the private state thread.
3204 struct PrivateStateThread {
3205 PrivateStateThread(
3206 Process &process, lldb::StateType public_state,
3207 lldb::StateType private_state,
3208 bool is_secondary_thread, // FIXME: Can I get rid of this?
3209 llvm::StringRef thread_name)
3210 : m_process(process), m_public_state(public_state),
3211 m_private_state(private_state),
3212 m_is_secondary_thread(is_secondary_thread),
3213 m_thread_name(thread_name) {}
3214 // This returns false if we couldn't start up the thread. If that happens,
3215 // you won't be doing any debugging today.
3216 bool StartupThread();
3217
3218 bool IsOnThread(const HostThread &thread) const;
3219
3220 bool IsJoinable() { return m_private_state_thread.IsJoinable(); }
3221
3222 void JoinAndReset() {
3223 lldb::thread_result_t result = {};
3224 m_private_state_thread.Join(&result);
3225 m_private_state_thread.Reset();
3226 m_is_running = false;
3227 }
3228
3229 bool IsRunning() { return m_is_running; }
3230
3231 void SetThreadName(llvm::StringRef new_name) { m_thread_name = new_name; }
3232
3233 lldb::StateType GetPrivateState() const {
3234 return m_private_state.GetValue();
3235 }
3236
3237 lldb::StateType GetPublicState() const { return m_public_state.GetValue(); }
3238
3239 void SetPublicState(lldb::StateType new_value) {
3240 m_public_state.SetValue(new_value);
3241 }
3242
3243 void SetPrivateState(lldb::StateType new_value) {
3244 m_private_state.SetValue(new_value);
3245 }
3246
3247 std::recursive_mutex &GetPrivateStateMutex() {
3248 return m_private_state.GetMutex();
3249 }
3250
3251 lldb::StateType GetPrivateStateNoLock() const {
3252 return m_private_state.GetValueNoLock();
3253 }
3254
3255 void SetPrivateStateNoLock(lldb::StateType new_state) {
3256 m_private_state.SetValueNoLock(new_state);
3257 }
3258
3259 void SetPublicStateNoLock(lldb::StateType new_state) {
3260 m_public_state.SetValueNoLock(new_state);
3261 }
3262
3263 bool SetPublicRunLockToRunning() { return m_public_run_lock.SetRunning(); }
3264
3265 bool SetPrivateRunLockToRunning() {
3266 return m_private_run_lock.SetRunning();
3267 }
3268
3269 bool SetPublicRunLockToStopped() { return m_public_run_lock.SetStopped(); }
3270
3271 bool SetPrivateRunLockToStopped() {
3272 return m_private_run_lock.SetStopped();
3273 }
3274
3275 ProcessRunLock &GetRunLock() {
3276 if (IsOnThread(Host::GetCurrentThread()))
3277 return m_private_run_lock;
3278 else
3279 return m_public_run_lock;
3280 }
3281
3282 Process &m_process;
3283 ///< The process state that we show to client code. This will often differ
3284 ///< from the actual process state, for instance when we've stopped in the
3285 ///< middle of a ThreadPlan's operations, before we've decided to stop or
3286 ///< continue.
3287 ThreadSafeValue<lldb::StateType> m_public_state;
3288 ///< The actual state of our process
3289 ThreadSafeValue<lldb::StateType> m_private_state;
3290 ///< HostThread for the thread that watches for internal state events
3291 HostThread m_private_state_thread;
3292 //< These are the locks that client code acquires both to wait on the
3293 //< process stopping, and then to ensure that it stays in the stopped state
3294 //< while the client code is operating on it. Again, we need a parallel
3295 //set, < one for public client code and one for code working on behalf of
3296 //the < private state management.
3297 ProcessRunLock m_public_run_lock;
3298 ProcessRunLock m_private_run_lock;
3299 bool m_is_running = false;
3300 /// If we need to run an expression modally in the private state thread, we
3301 /// have to spin up a secondary private state thread to manage the events
3302 /// that drive that interaction. This will be true if this is a modal
3303 /// private state thread.
3304 bool m_is_secondary_thread;
3305 ///< This will be the thread name given to the Private State HostThread when
3306 ///< it gets spun up.
3307 std::string m_thread_name;
3308 };
3309
3310 bool SetPrivateRunLockToStopped() {
3311 assert(m_current_private_state_thread);
3312 if (m_current_private_state_thread)
3313 return m_current_private_state_thread->SetPrivateRunLockToStopped();
3314 return false;
3315 }
3316 bool SetPrivateRunLockToRunning() {
3317 assert(m_current_private_state_thread);
3318 if (m_current_private_state_thread)
3319 return m_current_private_state_thread->SetPrivateRunLockToStopped();
3320 return false;
3321 }
3322 bool SetPublicRunLockToStopped() {
3323 assert(m_current_private_state_thread);
3324 if (m_current_private_state_thread)
3325 return m_current_private_state_thread->SetPublicRunLockToStopped();
3326 return false;
3327 }
3328 bool SetPublicRunLockToRunning() {
3329 assert(m_current_private_state_thread);
3330 if (m_current_private_state_thread)
3331 return m_current_private_state_thread->SetPublicRunLockToRunning();
3332 return false;
3333 }
3334
3335 std::recursive_mutex &GetPrivateStateMutex() {
3336 assert(m_current_private_state_thread);
3337 return m_current_private_state_thread->GetPrivateStateMutex();
3338 }
3339
3340 lldb::StateType GetPublicState() const {
3341 if (!m_current_private_state_thread)
3342 return lldb::eStateUnloaded;
3343 return m_current_private_state_thread->GetPublicState();
3344 }
3345
3346 lldb::StateType GetPrivateState() const {
3347 if (!m_current_private_state_thread)
3348 return lldb::eStateUnloaded;
3349 return m_current_private_state_thread->GetPrivateState();
3350 }
3351
3352 lldb::StateType GetPrivateStateNoLock() const {
3353 if (!m_current_private_state_thread)
3354 return lldb::eStateUnloaded;
3355 return m_current_private_state_thread->GetPrivateStateNoLock();
3356 }
3357
3358 void SetPrivateStateNoLock(lldb::StateType new_state) {
3359 assert(m_current_private_state_thread);
3360 m_current_private_state_thread->SetPrivateStateNoLock(new_state);
3361 }
3362
3363 // Member variables
3364 std::weak_ptr<Target> m_target_wp; ///< The target that owns this process.
3365 lldb::pid_t m_pid = LLDB_INVALID_PROCESS_ID;
3366 Broadcaster m_private_state_broadcaster; // This broadcaster feeds state
3367 // changed events into the private
3368 // state thread's listener.
3369 Broadcaster m_private_state_control_broadcaster; // This is the control
3370 // broadcaster, used to
3371 // pause, resume & stop the
3372 // private state thread.
3373 lldb::ListenerSP m_private_state_listener_sp; // This is the listener for the
3374 // private state thread.
3375 ///< This is filled on construction with the "main" private state which will
3376 ///< be exposed to clients of this process. It won't have a running private
3377 ///< state thread until you call StartupThread. This needs to be a pointer
3378 ///< so I can transparently swap it out for the modal one, but there will
3379 ///< always be a private state thread in this slot.
3380 PrivateStateThread *m_current_private_state_thread;
3381
3382 ProcessModID m_mod_id; ///< Tracks the state of the process over stops and
3383 ///other alterations.
3384 uint32_t m_process_unique_id; ///< Each lldb_private::Process class that is
3385 ///created gets a unique integer ID that
3386 ///increments with each new instance
3387 uint32_t m_thread_index_id; ///< Each thread is created with a 1 based index
3388 ///that won't get re-used.
3389 std::map<uint64_t, uint32_t> m_thread_id_to_index_id_map;
3390 int m_exit_status; ///< The exit status of the process, or -1 if not set.
3391 std::string m_exit_string; ///< A textual description of why a process exited.
3392 std::mutex m_exit_status_mutex; ///< Mutex so m_exit_status m_exit_string can
3393 ///be safely accessed from multiple threads
3394 std::recursive_mutex m_thread_mutex;
3395 ThreadList m_thread_list_real; ///< The threads for this process as are known
3396 ///to the protocol we are debugging with
3397 ThreadList m_thread_list; ///< The threads for this process as the user will
3398 ///see them. This is usually the same as
3399 ///< m_thread_list_real, but might be different if there is an OS plug-in
3400 ///creating memory threads
3401 ThreadPlanStackMap m_thread_plans; ///< This is the list of thread plans for
3402 /// threads in m_thread_list, as well as
3403 /// threads we knew existed, but haven't
3404 /// determined that they have died yet.
3405 ThreadList
3406 m_extended_thread_list; ///< Constituent for extended threads that may be
3407 /// generated, cleared on natural stops
3408 lldb::RunDirection m_base_direction; ///< ThreadPlanBase run direction
3409 uint32_t m_extended_thread_stop_id; ///< The natural stop id when
3410 ///extended_thread_list was last updated
3411 QueueList
3412 m_queue_list; ///< The list of libdispatch queues at a given stop point
3413 uint32_t m_queue_list_stop_id; ///< The natural stop id when queue list was
3414 ///last fetched
3415 StopPointSiteList<lldb_private::WatchpointResource>
3416 m_watchpoint_resource_list; ///< Watchpoint resources currently in use.
3417 std::vector<Notifications> m_notifications; ///< The list of notifications
3418 ///that this process can deliver.
3419 std::vector<lldb::addr_t> m_image_tokens;
3420 StopPointSiteList<lldb_private::BreakpointSite>
3421 m_breakpoint_site_list; ///< This is the list of breakpoint
3422 /// locations we intend to insert in
3423 /// the target.
3424 lldb::DynamicLoaderUP m_dyld_up;
3425 lldb::JITLoaderListUP m_jit_loaders_up;
3426 lldb::DynamicCheckerFunctionsUP m_dynamic_checkers_up; ///< The functions used
3427 /// by the expression
3428 /// parser to validate
3429 /// data that
3430 /// expressions use.
3431 lldb::OperatingSystemUP m_os_up;
3432 lldb::SystemRuntimeUP m_system_runtime_up;
3433 lldb::UnixSignalsSP
3434 m_unix_signals_sp; /// This is the current signal set for this process.
3435 lldb::ABISP m_abi_sp;
3436 lldb::IOHandlerSP m_process_input_reader;
3437 mutable std::mutex m_process_input_reader_mutex;
3438 ThreadedCommunication m_stdio_communication;
3439 std::recursive_mutex m_stdio_communication_mutex;
3440 bool m_stdin_forward; /// Remember if stdin must be forwarded to remote debug
3441 /// server
3442 std::string m_stdout_data;
3443 std::string m_stderr_data;
3444 std::recursive_mutex m_profile_data_comm_mutex;
3445 std::vector<std::string> m_profile_data;
3446 Predicate<uint32_t> m_iohandler_sync;
3447 MemoryCache m_memory_cache;
3448 AllocatedMemoryCache m_allocated_memory_cache;
3449 bool m_should_detach; /// Should we detach if the process object goes away
3450 /// with an explicit call to Kill or Detach?
3451 LanguageRuntimeCollection m_language_runtimes;
3452 std::recursive_mutex m_language_runtimes_mutex;
3453 InstrumentationRuntimeCollection m_instrumentation_runtimes;
3454 std::unique_ptr<NextEventAction> m_next_event_action_up;
3455 std::vector<PreResumeCallbackAndBaton> m_pre_resume_actions;
3456 bool m_currently_handling_do_on_removals;
3457 bool m_resume_requested; // If m_currently_handling_event or
3458 // m_currently_handling_do_on_removals are true,
3459 // Resume will only request a resume, using this
3460 // flag to check.
3461
3462 lldb::tid_t m_interrupt_tid; /// The tid of the thread that issued the async
3463 /// interrupt, used by thread plan timeout. It
3464 /// can be LLDB_INVALID_THREAD_ID to indicate
3465 /// user level async interrupt.
3466
3467 /// This is set at the beginning of Process::Finalize() to stop functions
3468 /// from looking up or creating things during or after a finalize call.
3469 std::atomic<bool> m_finalizing;
3470 // When we are "Finalizing" we need to do some cleanup. But if the Finalize
3471 // call is coming in the Destructor, we can't do any actual work in the
3472 // process because that is likely to call "shared_from_this" which crashes
3473 // if run while destructing. We use this flag to determine that.
3474 std::atomic<bool> m_destructing;
3475
3476 /// Mask for code an data addresses.
3477 /// The default value LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS_MASK means no mask has been set,
3478 /// and addresses values should not be modified.
3479 /// In these masks, the bits are set to 1 indicate bits that are not
3480 /// significant for addressing.
3481 /// The highmem masks are for targets where we may have different masks
3482 /// for low memory versus high memory addresses, and they will be left
3483 /// as LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS_MASK normally, meaning the base masks
3484 /// should be applied to all addresses.
3485 /// @{
3486 lldb::addr_t m_code_address_mask = LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS_MASK;
3487 lldb::addr_t m_data_address_mask = LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS_MASK;
3488 lldb::addr_t m_highmem_code_address_mask = LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS_MASK;
3489 lldb::addr_t m_highmem_data_address_mask = LLDB_INVALID_ADDRESS_MASK;
3490 /// @}
3491
3492 bool m_clear_thread_plans_on_stop;
3493 bool m_force_next_event_delivery;
3494 lldb::StateType m_last_broadcast_state; /// This helps with the Public event
3495 /// coalescing in
3496 /// ShouldBroadcastEvent.
3497 std::map<lldb::addr_t, lldb::addr_t> m_resolved_indirect_addresses;
3498 bool m_destroy_in_process;
3499 bool m_can_interpret_function_calls; // Some targets, e.g the OSX kernel,
3500 // don't support the ability to modify
3501 // the stack.
3502 std::mutex m_run_thread_plan_lock;
3503 llvm::StringMap<lldb::StructuredDataPluginSP> m_structured_data_plugin_map;
3504
3505 enum { eCanJITDontKnow = 0, eCanJITYes, eCanJITNo } m_can_jit;
3506
3507 std::unique_ptr<UtilityFunction> m_dlopen_utility_func_up;
3508 llvm::once_flag m_dlopen_utility_func_flag_once;
3509
3510 /// Per process source file cache.
3511 SourceManager::SourceFileCache m_source_file_cache;
3512
3513 /// A repository for extra crash information, consulted in
3514 /// GetExtendedCrashInformation.
3515 StructuredData::DictionarySP m_crash_info_dict_sp;
3516
3517 size_t RemoveBreakpointOpcodesFromBuffer(lldb::addr_t addr, size_t size,
3518 uint8_t *buf) const;
3519
3520 void SynchronouslyNotifyStateChanged(lldb::StateType state);
3521
3522 void SetPublicState(lldb::StateType new_state, bool restarted);
3523
3524 void SetPrivateState(lldb::StateType state);
3525
3526 // Starts the private state thread and assigns it to
3527 // m_current_private_state_thread. Clients who are making a secondary thread
3528 // for now have to manage backing that up by hand.
3529 bool StartPrivateStateThread(lldb::StateType state, bool run_lock_is_running,
3530 bool is_secondary_thread = false);
3531
3532 void StopPrivateStateThread();
3533
3534 void PausePrivateStateThread();
3535
3536 void ResumePrivateStateThread();
3537
3538private:
3539 // The starts up the private state thread that will watch for events from the
3540 // debugee. Pass true for is_secondary_thread in the case where you have to
3541 // temporarily spin up a secondary state thread to handle events from a hand-
3542 // called function on the primary private state thread.
3543
3544 lldb::thread_result_t RunPrivateStateThread();
3545
3546protected:
3547 void HandlePrivateEvent(lldb::EventSP &event_sp);
3548
3549 Status HaltPrivate();
3550
3551 lldb::StateType WaitForProcessStopPrivate(lldb::EventSP &event_sp,
3552 const Timeout<std::micro> &timeout);
3553
3554 // This waits for both the state change broadcaster, and the control
3555 // broadcaster. If control_only, it only waits for the control broadcaster.
3556
3557 bool GetEventsPrivate(lldb::EventSP &event_sp,
3558 const Timeout<std::micro> &timeout, bool control_only);
3559
3560 lldb::StateType
3561 GetStateChangedEventsPrivate(lldb::EventSP &event_sp,
3562 const Timeout<std::micro> &timeout);
3563
3564 size_t WriteMemoryPrivate(lldb::addr_t addr, const void *buf, size_t size,
3565 Status &error);
3566
3567 void AppendSTDOUT(const char *s, size_t len);
3568
3569 void AppendSTDERR(const char *s, size_t len);
3570
3571 void BroadcastAsyncProfileData(const std::string &one_profile_data);
3572
3573 static void STDIOReadThreadBytesReceived(void *baton, const void *src,
3574 size_t src_len);
3575
3576 bool PushProcessIOHandler();
3577
3578 bool PopProcessIOHandler();
3579
3580 bool ProcessIOHandlerIsActive();
3581
3582 bool ProcessIOHandlerExists() const {
3583 std::lock_guard<std::mutex> guard(m_process_input_reader_mutex);
3584 return static_cast<bool>(m_process_input_reader);
3585 }
3586
3587 Status StopForDestroyOrDetach(lldb::EventSP &exit_event_sp);
3588
3589 virtual Status UpdateAutomaticSignalFiltering();
3590
3591 void LoadOperatingSystemPlugin(bool flush);
3592
3593 void SetAddressableBitMasks(AddressableBits bit_masks);
3594
3595private:
3596 Status DestroyImpl(bool force_kill);
3597
3598 /// This is the part of the event handling that for a process event. It
3599 /// decides what to do with the event and returns true if the event needs to
3600 /// be propagated to the user, and false otherwise. If the event is not
3601 /// propagated, this call will most likely set the target to executing
3602 /// again. There is only one place where this call should be called,
3603 /// HandlePrivateEvent. Don't call it from anywhere else...
3604 ///
3605 /// \param[in] event_ptr
3606 /// This is the event we are handling.
3607 ///
3608 /// \return
3609 /// Returns \b true if the event should be reported to the
3610 /// user, \b false otherwise.
3611 bool ShouldBroadcastEvent(Event *event_ptr);
3612
3613 void ControlPrivateStateThread(uint32_t signal);
3614
3615 Status LaunchPrivate(ProcessLaunchInfo &launch_info, lldb::StateType &state,
3616 lldb::EventSP &event_sp);
3617
3618 lldb::EventSP CreateEventFromProcessState(uint32_t event_type);
3619
3620 Process(const Process &) = delete;
3621 const Process &operator=(const Process &) = delete;
3622};
3623
3624/// RAII guard that should be acquired when an utility function is called within
3625/// a given process.
3626class UtilityFunctionScope {
3627 Process *m_process;
3628
3629public:
3630 UtilityFunctionScope(Process *p) : m_process(p) {
3631 if (m_process)
3632 m_process->SetRunningUtilityFunction(true);
3633 }
3634 ~UtilityFunctionScope() {
3635 if (m_process)
3636 m_process->SetRunningUtilityFunction(false);
3637 }
3638};
3639
3640} // namespace lldb_private
